skill.name: Ruby Pro
skill.description: Write idiomatic Ruby code with metaprogramming, Rails patterns, and performance optimization. Specializes in Ruby on Rails, gem development, and testing frameworks.
skill.summary: Expert Ruby programming assistant for clean, maintainable, and performant Ruby code.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill containing instructional text for Ruby programming assistance. Static analysis scanned 0 files with 0 risk patterns detected. No executable code, network access, filesystem operations, or external command execution present. The skill provides guidance on Ruby idioms, Rails patterns, and testing practices without any security concerns.

content.user_title: Master Ruby Development with Expert Guidance
content.value_statement: Ruby developers struggle with writing idiomatic code and following best practices. This skill provides expert guidance on metaprogramming, Rails patterns, and performance optimization.
actual_capabilities: Generate idiomatic Ruby code following community conventions
Apply Rails patterns for ActiveRecord, controllers, and views
Design Ruby gems with proper dependency management and versioning
Write comprehensive RSpec and Minitest test suites
Optimize Ruby code performance with profiling and benchmarking
Refactor legacy Ruby code for improved readability and maintainability
limitations: Cannot execute Ruby code or run tests directly
Cannot access external Ruby documentation or gem repositories
Cannot profile or benchmark code in your actual runtime environment
Recommendations are based on general best practices, not your specific application context

use_cases.title: Rails Application Development
use_cases.description: Build robust Rails applications using MVC architecture, ActiveRecord patterns, and RESTful controllers.

use_cases.title: Gem Library Creation
use_cases.description: Develop and publish Ruby gems with proper specifications, versioning, and dependency management.
use_cases.target_user: Ruby library authors and open source contributors

use_cases.title: Code Quality Improvement
use_cases.description: Refactor existing Ruby codebases to follow idioms, improve performance, and pass RuboCop analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Ruby Method
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Ruby method that takes an array of integers and returns the sum of all even numbers. Follow Ruby conventions and include edge case handling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Learning basic Ruby syntax and enumerables

prompt_templates.title: Rails Model Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ActiveRecord model for a blog post with associations to user and comments. Include validations, scopes, and a custom instance method for generating a slug.
prompt_templates.scenario: Building Rails applications with proper model architecture

prompt_templates.title: Metaprogramming DSL
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Ruby DSL using metaprogramming that allows defining configuration options with type validation. Use class macros and method_missing appropriately.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced Ruby metaprogramming for library development

prompt_templates.title: RSpec Test Suite
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a comprehensive RSpec test suite for a service class that processes payments. Include unit tests with mocks, integration tests, and edge cases for failure scenarios.

output_examples.input: Create a Ruby method to validate email format
output_examples.output: A reusable email validation method using regex with proper Unicode support, following Ruby naming conventions and returning boolean values.
Refactored query using eager loading with includes, select optimization, and database indexing recommendations to reduce N+1 queries.

output_examples.input: Write RSpec tests for a Rails controller
output_examples.output: Complete controller spec with request tests, response status assertions, JSON payload validation, and authentication edge cases.

best_practices: Follow Ruby naming conventions: snake_case for methods, CamelCase for classes, and descriptive variable names
Use blocks and enumerables instead of manual loops for cleaner, more expressive code
Apply the principle of least surprise by following Ruby and Rails community conventions
anti_patterns: Avoid excessive metaprogramming that makes code difficult to understand and debug
Do not ignore return values or suppress exceptions without proper handling
Never commit without running tests and RuboCop analysis on your changes

faq.question: What Ruby versions does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill covers Ruby 2.7 through Ruby 3.3, including modern features like pattern matching, endless methods, and rightward assignment.

faq.question: Can this skill help with Rails-specific issues?
faq.answer: Yes, it specializes in Rails patterns including ActiveRecord queries, controller best practices, view helpers, and background job processing.

faq.question: Does this skill write tests automatically?
faq.answer: The skill provides test code examples and guidance for RSpec and Minitest, but you must run and maintain the tests in your environment.

faq.question: Can it help optimize slow Ruby code?
faq.answer: Yes, it can suggest performance improvements, identify bottlenecks, and recommend profiling tools like benchmark-ips and StackProf.

faq.question: What about gem development and publishing?
faq.answer: It covers gemspec creation, dependency management, semantic versioning, and best practices for publishing to RubyGems.

faq.question: Does this skill replace RuboCop or other static analysis tools?
faq.answer: No, it complements static analysis tools by providing contextual guidance and explaining the reasoning behind code quality rules.
